title: Where the Adding-Fractions Formula Comes From

md: Many students memorize a formula for adding fractions:

It looks like a magic spell — multiply *this* by *that*, add, put it all over $bd$. But it isn't magic. It's the only thing that *could* happen once you insist the two fractions be cut into matching pieces.
### Step 1 — You can't add unlike pieces

$\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ are pieces of **different sizes**. Adding their numerators ($1 + 1$) would be nonsense — like saying "one slice plus one slice" when the slices aren't the same. First, both fractions must be re-cut into pieces of *one common size*.
### Step 2 — A common size that always works: $bd$

Cut every $b$-th into $d$ smaller pieces, and every $d$-th into $b$ smaller pieces. Now both are measured in **$bd$-ths**. Using equivalent fractions (multiply top and bottom by the same number — the value doesn't change):
### Step 3 — Now the pieces match, so just count them
Both fractions are now made of $bd$-ths. One contributes $ad$ of those pieces, the other contributes $bc$. Adding is now plain counting:
The "formula" was never a separate rule — it's just *find a common denominator, then count*. The picture below does exactly this: it re-slices both fractions into a shared grid so the pieces line up.

blocks.title: A note on the LCM
blocks.body: $bd$ always works, but it isn't always the *smallest* common denominator. For $\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}$, the formula gives $24$ths, but $12$ths (the LCM) is tidier. Same answer either way — using the LCM just saves you a final simplification.
